title: Engineering Practice
description: Engineering and project skills from scratch for complete beginners (in progress): the universal habits behind building anything well — systems thinking and requirements, the design process, trade-offs, specs, measurement, debugging, testing, version control, technical communication, project management, safety and ethics, and modern AI-assisted workflows. Beginner-first readings, a diagram in nearly every lesson, MCQ practice, tiered easy to hard.

lessons.title: What Is a System?
lessons.content:
Think about a car. It has an engine, wheels, brakes, and a steering wheel. On their own, none of those parts can take you to the shops. Put them together so they work as a team, and suddenly you can drive. That teamwork is the whole idea behind a **system**.

A **system** is a set of parts that work together to do something the parts cannot do alone. A kitchen during a busy dinner rush is a system: cooks, ovens, orders, and waiters all working together to get meals out. A sports team is a system too, where the lineup and how players pass the ball often matter more than any single star.

Two things make a system more than just a pile of parts. The first is the **interfaces**: the connections where parts meet and hand things off to each other. In a car, the pedal connects to the brakes; in a kitchen, the order slip connects the waiter to the cook. The second is the **boundary**: the imaginary line that says where your system ends and the outside world (its **environment**) begins. The car is the system; the road, weather, and driver are its environment.

When parts combine, the system can behave in ways no single part does. We call this **emergent behavior**. A car *moving* is emergent: no bolt or wheel "moves you" by itself. The catch: because the parts are connected, fixing one part can quietly break another. That is why it pays to see the whole, not just the piece in front of you.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes what a 'system' is?
choices: The single most important part of a machine
A set of parts that work together to do something the parts cannot do alone
Any object that is large and complicated
A list of instructions for one person to follow
items.explanation: A system is parts working together toward a shared purpose, like an engine, wheels, and brakes making a car drive. It is not just one important part (the point is the teamwork), and size or complexity alone does not make something a system. A plain instruction list is not a set of connected parts.

items.prompt: A car can drive you somewhere even though no single bolt or wheel can move you by itself. What is this 'whole behaves differently than its parts' idea called?
choices: An interface
A boundary
Emergent behavior
An assumption
items.explanation: Emergent behavior is when the whole system does something none of its parts do alone, like a car moving. An interface is where parts connect, and a boundary is where the system ends; neither names the 'whole vs parts' effect. An assumption is something taken for granted, which is unrelated here.

items.prompt: In a kitchen during a dinner rush, the order slip that the waiter hands to the cook is an example of what?
choices: The boundary of the system
The environment outside the system
Emergent behavior of the kitchen
An interface, where two parts connect and hand something off
items.explanation: An interface is the connection point where parts meet and pass something between them, exactly what the order slip does between waiter and cook. The boundary is where the system ends, the environment is the outside world, and emergent behavior is what the whole produces; none of those describe a hand-off point.

items.prompt: You decide to study a coffee machine as your system. The kitchen counter, the wall outlet, and the person pressing the button are best described as what?
choices: The environment, outside the system's boundary
Parts inside the system
Interfaces inside the system
Emergent behaviors of the system
items.explanation: Anything outside the line you draw around your system is its environment. If the coffee machine is the system, the counter, outlet, and user sit in the environment. They are not internal parts or interfaces of the machine, and they are certainly not behaviors that emerge from it.

items.prompt: What does the 'boundary' of a system tell you?
choices: Which part of the system is the most expensive
How fast the system can run
Where the system ends and the outside environment begins
The total number of parts in the system
items.explanation: A boundary is the dividing line between what counts as your system and what counts as the outside world. It says nothing about cost, speed, or how many parts there are; those are separate questions. Drawing the boundary clearly is what lets you say what is 'in' versus 'out'.

items.prompt: Why is 'seeing the whole system' useful when you change one part?
choices: Because the biggest part is always the cause of any problem
Because parts are connected, so fixing one part can accidentally break another
Because parts never affect each other once assembled
Because a system always works better with fewer parts
items.explanation: Parts connect through interfaces, so a change in one place can ripple out and break something elsewhere; seeing the whole helps you catch that. It is false that parts never affect each other (the opposite is the danger), that the biggest part is always to blame, or that fewer parts is automatically better.

items.prompt: Why can a sports team with weaker individual players sometimes beat a team of stars?
choices: The number of players on each side is always different
Individual skill is the only thing that ever decides a game
A team is not a system, so teamwork does not apply
How the parts connect and work together can matter more than any single part
items.explanation: A team is a system, and how players pass and coordinate (the connections between parts) can produce results no single star delivers. Saying skill is the only factor ignores teamwork, the player counts are the same, and a team clearly is a system of parts working together.

items.prompt: Which of these is the clearest example of emergent behavior?
choices: A single battery sitting on a shelf
A clock telling the time, even though no single gear 'tells time' alone
A screw lying loose in a drawer
A wheel by itself rolling down a hill once pushed
items.explanation: Telling the time emerges only when the gears, hands, and spring work together; no lone gear does it. A battery on a shelf, a loose screw, and a single wheel are each just one part doing (or not doing) its own simple thing, with no whole-system behavior emerging.

items.prompt: An engineer says, 'Let's define the system of interest before we start.' What are they mainly trying to settle?
choices: Which single part should be painted first
How much the whole project will be sold for
Who on the team is the most experienced
Which parts are inside the boundary and which things are part of the environment
items.explanation: Defining the system of interest means drawing the boundary: deciding what is in (the parts you will work on) and what is out (the environment). It is not about paint order, sale price, or who is most experienced; those do not tell you where the system begins and ends.

lessons.title: From a Vague Wish to Clear Requirements
lessons.content:
Imagine telling a builder, "I want a nice kitchen." You will probably get something random and not what you pictured. Now imagine saying, "Counters at 90 cm high, an oven that fits a large baking tray, finished by June." Now you are far more likely to get what you wanted. The difference is **requirements**.

Before building anything, a **requirement** is a clear, specific statement of *what the thing must do or be*. Good requirements turn a vague wish ("I want a good website") into concrete, testable statements ("loads in under 2 seconds," "works on a phone"). They are the heart of getting the right result.

Requirements come in a few kinds, and telling them apart helps. A **functional** requirement says what the thing must *do*, an action: "let users reset their password." A **non-functional** requirement describes a *quality* of how well it does things: speed, reliability, cost, or safety, like "loads in under 2 seconds." A **constraint** is a fixed limit you cannot change: "the budget is $500" or "it must run on a phone." Finally, an **assumption** is something you are taking for granted without proof, such as "users will have an internet connection."

A common beginner trap is mixing these up, especially calling a quality (non-functional) a feature (functional). Another trap is leaving a requirement vague: "it should be fast" sounds fine but cannot be checked, because nobody knows what "fast" means. The fix, which we sharpen next, is to make every requirement specific enough to test.

items.prompt: What is a 'requirement' for a project?
choices: A rough guess about how long the project will take
A clear, specific statement of what the thing must do or be
The name of the person paying for the project
A drawing of the finished product's color scheme
items.explanation: A requirement states what the thing must do or be, clearly and specifically, so you can build the right result and later check it. A time guess, the sponsor's name, and a color drawing are all other things; none of them pin down what the product must actually accomplish.

items.prompt: Why is 'I want a good website' a poor requirement on its own?
choices: It is vague, so you cannot check whether it was actually met
It is too long and detailed to be useful
It names a specific technology too early
It states a fixed budget that cannot change
items.explanation: The word 'good' is vague, so there is no way to test whether the result counts as good; that is the core problem. It is the opposite of too detailed, it names no technology, and it states no budget. A useful version replaces 'good' with specific, checkable statements.

items.prompt: Classify this requirement: 'The app shall let a user reset their password.'
choices: Non-functional (a quality like speed or reliability)
A constraint (a fixed limit)
Functional (something the app must do)
An assumption (something taken for granted)
items.explanation: Resetting a password is an action the app must perform, so it is a functional requirement. It is not describing a quality such as speed (non-functional), it is not a fixed limit like a budget (constraint), and it is not something merely assumed to be true.

items.prompt: Classify this requirement: 'The page shall load in under 2 seconds.'
choices: Functional (an action it must do)
Non-functional (a quality of how well it performs)
A constraint (a fixed external limit)
An assumption (something taken for granted)
items.explanation: Loading speed describes how well the page performs, which is a quality, so it is non-functional. It is a common trap to call this functional, but it is not a new action the page does; it is about the quality of an action. It is also not a fixed limit you were handed, nor an assumption.

items.prompt: Classify this requirement: 'The total budget must not exceed $500.'
choices: Functional (an action the product does)
Non-functional (a performance quality)
A constraint (a fixed limit you cannot change)
An assumption (something taken for granted)
items.explanation: A fixed budget cap is a limit imposed on the project, so it is a constraint. It is not something the product does (functional) and not a quality of how it performs (non-functional). It is also stated as a hard rule, not merely assumed, so it is not an assumption.

items.prompt: Which is the clearest difference between a functional and a non-functional requirement?
choices: Functional is always written down; non-functional never is
Functional says what it must do; non-functional says how well it must do it
Functional is optional; non-functional is mandatory
There is no real difference between them
items.explanation: Functional requirements name actions (what it does), while non-functional ones describe qualities like speed or reliability (how well it does them). Both should be written down, neither is automatically optional or mandatory because of its type, and the two are genuinely different categories.

items.prompt: 'We assume every user will have a reliable internet connection.' What kind of statement is this?
choices: A functional requirement (an action to build)
A non-functional requirement (a quality to hit)
A constraint set by the budget
An assumption (something taken for granted without proof)
items.explanation: This is something taken as true without being guaranteed, which is exactly an assumption. It is not an action the product performs, not a measured quality of performance, and not a budget-imposed limit. Writing assumptions down matters because if one turns out false, the plan may break.

items.prompt: Which rewrite best turns the vague wish 'make it easy to use' into a usable requirement?
choices: It should feel friendly and modern
A new user shall complete sign-up in 3 steps or fewer
It must be the easiest app anyone has ever used
It should just be easy, like everyone expects
items.explanation: 'Complete sign-up in 3 steps or fewer' is specific and can be checked by counting steps. The others stay vague: 'friendly and modern', 'easiest ever', and 'just easy' all leave you with no clear way to tell whether the result passes or fails.

items.prompt: A teammate says, 'It should be fast, everyone knows what that means, so the requirement is fine.' What is the best response?
choices: Agreed, vague requirements are perfectly okay if they sound reasonable
Agreed, since speed is a quality it does not need to be specific
It is not fine yet; 'fast' is vague and must be made specific so it can be tested
It is fine because everyone will interpret 'fast' the same way
items.explanation: 'Fast' means different things to different people, so the requirement cannot be checked until it is made specific, such as 'loads in under 2 seconds'. Accepting a vague requirement, or assuming everyone agrees on 'fast', is the trap; qualities like speed especially need a concrete, testable number.

lessons.title: Good Requirements, Acceptance Tests & Traceability
lessons.content:
You now know what requirements are. This lesson is about making them *good*, and proving they were met.

A **good requirement** passes a quick checklist. It is **clear** (no room for two readings), **verifiable** (you can test whether it is met), **atomic** (it states one thing, not five), **feasible** (actually possible), and **necessary** (you really need it). A vague line like "it should be fast" fails because it is not verifiable.

A big pitfall is **solution-bias**: writing *how* to build something instead of *what* it must achieve. "Shall show the time remaining" says what (good); "shall use a red LED display" says how (bad), because it locks in one solution before you have compared options. Compare "get to work by 9" (the real need) with "buy a red sports car" (one premature solution). Always state *what*, not *how*.

Requirements use careful words. **Shall** means it is mandatory (a must). **Should** means preferred but not required. **May** means optional. Mixing these up, like thinking "should" means mandatory, leads to building the wrong things.

Every requirement also needs an **acceptance test**: a concrete way to prove it is met. For "loads in under 2 seconds," the test is "time the load and confirm it is under 2 seconds." Finally, **traceability** means linking each requirement to the design choice that addresses it and the test that proves it, like a checklist where every must-have points to its proof, so nothing is forgotten. The same idea scales to **budgets as requirements**: one big limit, such as a $500 total or a 2-second load time, is split among the parts. Together these habits stop you from building the wrong thing well.

items.prompt: Which set of qualities describes a GOOD requirement?
choices: Long, detailed, and full of technical words
Vague enough to allow any interpretation later
Clear, verifiable, atomic, feasible, and necessary
Written only after the product is finished
items.explanation: A good requirement is clear, verifiable (testable), atomic (one thing), feasible, and necessary. Length and jargon do not make it good, vagueness makes it worse (you cannot test it), and requirements are written before building, not after.

items.prompt: What does it mean for a requirement to be 'verifiable'?
choices: There is a concrete way to test whether it has been met
It has been approved by a manager
It sounds reasonable to most people
It is written in formal-sounding language
items.explanation: Verifiable means you can actually test it and get a yes or no, which is why specific, measurable wording matters. Manager approval, sounding reasonable, or using formal language do not make a requirement testable; only a concrete check does. Thinking an untestable requirement is acceptable is the trap to avoid.

items.prompt: Which requirement shows solution-bias (says HOW instead of WHAT)?
choices: The timer shall show the time remaining
The timer shall be readable from across the room
The timer shall use a red LED display
The timer shall start when the start button is pressed
items.explanation: 'Use a red LED display' dictates one specific solution (the how) rather than the goal, which is solution-bias. The others state what must be achieved (show time remaining, be readable, start on a press) and leave the designer free to pick the best way to do it.

items.prompt: Why is it risky to lock in a solution too early inside a requirement?
choices: It always makes the product cost less
It can rule out better solutions you have not considered yet
It guarantees the project finishes faster
It makes the requirement easier to test
items.explanation: Stating how (a specific solution) too early closes the door on potentially better options, which is the main danger of solution-bias. It does not reliably cut cost, speed up the project, or make testing easier; in fact, a 'what' requirement is usually just as testable and keeps your options open.

items.prompt: In requirement language, what does 'shall' mean?
choices: It is optional, take it or leave it
It is only a preference, not required
It is a guess about the future
It is mandatory, a must
items.explanation: 'Shall' marks a mandatory requirement, something that must be done. 'May' marks optional, and 'should' marks a preference that is not required. None of these words is a guess about the future; they set how binding each requirement is.

items.prompt: A teammate treats every 'should' in the spec as a strict must-have. Why is that a mistake?
choices: 'Should' means preferred, not mandatory; only 'shall' is mandatory
'Should' actually means optional, weaker than 'may'
'Should' and 'shall' mean exactly the same thing
'Should' means the requirement can be ignored entirely
items.explanation: 'Should' signals a preference that is not strictly required, while 'shall' is the mandatory word; treating 'should' as a must can waste effort on non-essentials. 'Should' is stronger than the optional 'may', it is not identical to 'shall', and it is not something to ignore outright.

items.prompt: What is an 'acceptance test' for a requirement?
choices: A meeting where the team votes on the design
A concrete way to prove the requirement has been met
A guess about whether users will like the product
The first draft of the requirement before edits
items.explanation: An acceptance test is the concrete check that proves a requirement was satisfied, such as timing a page load to confirm it is under 2 seconds. It is not a vote, a guess about user reactions, or a draft; it is the evidence that the requirement is truly met.

items.prompt: What does 'traceability' mean in requirements work?
choices: Tracking how many hours each person worked
Keeping a copy of every email about the project
Linking each requirement to the design choice addressing it and the test proving it
Drawing the product to exact scale
items.explanation: Traceability links every requirement to the design decision that addresses it and the test that proves it, so nothing gets forgotten, like a checklist tying each must-have to its proof. Timesheets, saved emails, and scale drawings are unrelated to connecting requirements through design to test.

items.prompt: You have a hard limit of $500 for a whole project. How does the idea of 'budgets as requirements' apply?
choices: Spend the full $500 on whichever part you build first
Ignore the limit until the project is finished
Treat the $500 as a vague suggestion, not a real limit
Split the $500 limit among the parts so each part gets its own share
items.explanation: A big limit is decomposed into smaller shares across the parts, just as a 2-second load time can be split across steps; that keeps the whole within bounds. Spending it all on the first part, ignoring the limit, or treating a hard cap as a mere suggestion all break the budget as a requirement.
